QR Code automatically generated for the product in wordpress admin.




​I took a picture of the QR code above, reverse engineered a web based scanner and presto it scanned and provided the url.. all I need to do from there is redirect to the product.



and while in the process of developing this I learned that if you open your camera app (as of iOS 11 / June 2017, and it looks like certain Android phones had the same capabilities over a year before) and aim it at a QR it prompts you to load the webpage..

normally it would say 'Paradigm Hydroseed' instead of 'Notification' but I took this screenshot while my phone was locked / private.



​
 easy as that!

​I think that given the last discovery, I wouldn't even bother with advertising / using a separate domain like I was thinking at lunch. Reason being is that I can prefix the URL in each QR code to automatically load the 'onsite' version of paradigmhydroseed.com. Once the visitor is in an 'onsite session' I can then include the web based scanner I developed earlier so that the visitor does not have to leave the website and open the camera again.. totally streamlined.

Onsite I would go with a placard with the product name, price, description, instructions (open camera, point camera), etc and include something like this to indicate.





​Thats gotta cover your ass for ​at least ​95% of people that have the ability to drive onto your yard​ and have a phone.

I wasn't aware but that form that was made by Stratus Hub doesn't validate against salesforce it simply adds the lead to SF regardless of if the code validates.

I propose:

1. users are imported to wordpress with a code (email can go out too if we have it for user) but if we do not have email for user will need to have a dummy address as all WP accounts need an email.
2. the form starts with just the code (it can be made a little more complex)
3. if it validates it takes them to their account details (not sure what is present for data at this stage, but whatever there is can be auto-populated / presented for verification also can collect additional fields). If it fails error message and returns to code input.
4. the tricky part will be if user doesn't have an email the dummy email field will need to be cleared from their account info or simple solution is to just put a message to say 'please update email'

edit: I just found out (95% sure) I can remove the dummy emails however not sure if it can be done programmatically which would likely have to be during import. I have contacted the support ppl for the importer to see if its possible to import without an email.

I didn't want to CC you and look like a snitch but just FYI I spent an hour cleaning this up.. ---------- Forwarded message ---------- From: Thomas Senecal Date: Tue, Mar 22, 2016 at 10:40 PM Subject: CSS / VIP / edits / fixes To: Media SCOPE Art Show hey David, a few things.. This is how to start a new content section / layer. This evolved during the development of the ERC, particularly the curatorial page. Content sections come and go ad nauseam on curatorial page so this alternates the background image and colors of text, strokes on field input, button colors and so on based on the order in the stack.
class="layer" style="background: url(/img/gradient-grey.jpg) no-repeat center; -webkit-background-size: cover; -moz-background-size: cover; -o-background-size: cover; background-size: cover;"class="layer alt" style="background: url(/img/gradient-black.jpg) no-repeat center; -webkit-background-size: cover; -moz-background-size: cover; -o-background-size: cover; background-size: cover;">
where the content goes.
the alt (alternate) class is the key, it changes all the colors. the above practice is not in effect outside of the ERC but it should be used / applied regardless of the styling as that can be adjusted, just need the 'dynamic-ness' of it. I removed this CSS from /vip #viploginform .login-submit .button-primary {background: #3c3c3c!important} #viploginform input[type="text"], #viploginform input[type="password"] {border: 2px solid #000; border-color: #000 !important; color: #000;} whenever possible remove CSS (what I did, from main.css) as opposed to adding.. it causes bloat and can render differently in certain browsers. by removing that from the page and #viploginform selector from main.css everything

ok, ill get this finished up in the next couple days,

for website pics, generally you don't need to get too crazy.. web resolution is pretty low compared to print.

for a full screen image (like a background, or the CARE homepage) I generally stick to 1600px wide at 72dpi (web resolution) and I down sample to 30-40% in photoshop which drastically reduces file size without sacrificing too much quality (jpg only).

If you're doing something like employee headshots, I would do about 600px wide. with retina displays and touch device zoom you want content images to be about twice the size they display.

good luck!

Hi V,

Im not sure how Muse works, I've found that the best practice is to avoid anything that gets between you and the website source code as it teaches you to learn the app instead of the code.

I wondered... is it possible to do this with Wordpress

theres a couple ways to tackle this, they're not wordpress specific. CSS manages the way the elements on a website look and CSS supports screen resolution queries so you can check if a visitor is on a certain size screen and adjust the site accordingly. You can also setup a mobile specific site that delivers different content to mobile viewers, usually simplified..

as for developing an app, a true 'app' needs to be submit to review to apple / android and is not just a mobile version of your website. It requires download and it should have additional functionality. users shouldn't have to download an app to view an adjusted version of your website, it can be done properly in a browser.

hi Renato, I took care of this update as well as all other plugins, only took about 15 minutes.

---------- Forwarded message ----------
From: The Envato Team 
Date: Mon, Oct 19, 2015 at 9:48 AM
Subject: Envato Market Security Notice: Boxit
To: eliteweblabs 


 
Envato Account
Hello eliteweblabs,

We are getting in touch to let you know about a security vulnerability in the BOXIT - The Dropbox file upload for Wordpress plugin versions prior to 2.7 (releases prior to October 19, 2015).

This is a severe vulnerability and you should take action now.

We have been working with Sommersethdesign, the creators of Boxit, who have addressed this vulnerability.

What You Should Do

In order to secure your website from this vulnerability, we strongly encourage you to update to version 2.7 or later as soon as possible.

You can update Boxit through the Plugins screen in WordPress. It should automatically detect the new version and show a prompt similar to the one in this screenshot: http://envato.d.pr/bMwW/4Pl5gUyp. Click "update now" to update.

If Boxit is not activated, you will not see the prompt to update to the new version. However, you are still vulnerable. Please either delete Boxit from your server or activate it and update to 2.7.

If Boxit is activated but for some reason the update prompt does not appear, you will need to download the item manually from CodeCanyon and upload it to your server. Log into CodeCanyon and go your Downloads, locate Boxit and download it. Unzip the file and FTP the boxit folder to wp-content/plugins/, overwriting the existing files. 

You should double check whether you have updated successfully by checking the version number for Boxit on the Plugins screen in WordPress. Please make sure that the version number says 2.7 or higher.

If you have used this plugin in projects for clients, please help them to secure their sites as well.

From: Flywheel Support <help@getflywheel.com>
Date: Wed, Sep 9, 2015 at 12:37 PM
Subject: Re: Site is showing as hacked
To: sen@eliteweblabs.com


Hey Thomas,

Thanks for letting us know about your malware concerns for this site. We’re running some scans right now to see if we can spot any infected files. This can take some time, since it’s something that we do in several phases. Apologies for that in advance. We’ll be sure to let you know when everything looks clear on our end.

While we’re working on that, one thing that can be done on your end is updating all themes and plugins to their most recent version, uninstalling any that aren’t being used, as well as changing passwords on any user accounts to something that is as secure as possible. Since old plugin/theme versions and weak passwords are the primary ways that WordPress sites become infected with malware, taking care of this as soon as is possible will also help us to make sure that reinfection doesn’t happen as we clean. Let us know once that has been done, and we’ll run some final scans on the site to make sure everything was caught.

Let us know if you have any questions. We’re happy to answer them.

Thanks,
Aaron

WordPress™ hosting setup, WordPress™ installation and everything that comes along with migrating. hi Scott, see my responses below

1. Can we change the entire look of the site?

With all WordPress™ websites, the theme controls the style / behavior of the website in a major way so switching to a new theme is not a simple process, rebuilding would be required. 

2.  I was hoping to do different pages instead of on long page

Single Scroller style websites have become super prevalent in the last few years due to mobile devices. Scrolling is the new clicking and frankly the current RCE site was way ahead of its time and I see no reason to change it and this is coming from a guy who stands to make $ by saying the opposite. 

The only argument against SS style websites is Search Engine Optimization (SEO) and that is not even a concern for RCE, its already cornered the market.

3. . I am also looking to have separate pages for different aspects of the business.

not to be crass but do you have any reason why? I do this all day, every day and short of the owner saying 'because thats what I want' Ill handle the design.

4. Is there software that we can use to change the entire site or can it all be done with word press.

WordPress™ is the software that runs the site. also see my answer to #1

5. I am not a wiz at building sites but I use to use frontpage to build several sites.

frontpage is an editor that is vastly outdated, at least when compared to WordPress™ which is a Content Management System (CMS) that runs on PHP allowing for dynamic code as well as simple administration for the site owner. 

Simply put, if you use Frontpage you learn how to use Frontpage. 
If you use WordPress™, you learn how to make websites... and its all free!

Step 1: Google Developers Console

To use this integration you need create a project in Google Developers Console.

Step 2: Create Project

Click on Create Project button. A popup should appear asking for Project Name and Project ID. These are set by you and can be anything you’d like.

Step 3: Enable the Calendar API

Once the project has been created, go back to the Projects screen and select your project. Within your project, go to APIs & auth link on the left side menu. The menu will expand to provide you more options, click on the link for APIs. Locate Calendar API and enable it.

Step 4: Setup your Consent Screen options

Go to API’s & auth > Consent screen, now you only need fill the PRODUCT NAME field:

google-calendar-consent-screen

Note that you can use any name, but it is mandatory to fill this field.

Step 5: Create Credentials

Under the APIs & auth link menu on the left, click on Credentials. Then click on Create new Client ID.

Step 6: Insert New Credentials

Application type should be Web.

Authorized JavaScript Origins can be blank or your domain.

Set the Authorized redirect URI as http://example.com/wc-api/wc_bookings_google_calendar/. Where example.com is should be your domain.

Step 7: Copy Credentials to your WordPress settings

Navigate to WooCommerce > Settings> Integration > Google Calendar on your WordPress site. Copy and paste the credentials that were just provided to you from Google into your WordPress site. This will include your Client ID and Client Secret.

Step 8: Google Calendar ID

If you haven’t done so already, create the calendar you’ll be using in Google Calendar. Once your calendar is created, navigate to My calendars menu on the left and select Calendar Settings.

google_calendar_settings

On this screen you will see a section called Calendar Address. Copy the Calendar ID provided to you and insert it into your WooCommerce settings from Step 6 under Calendar ID. Now press Save.
